Facts.text: Felix I begins his reign as Catholic Pope
French poet Francois Villon banished from Paris
Felix Manz, a leader of the Anabaptist congregation in Zürich, is executed by drowning. (b. 1498)
Great fire in Eindhoven, Netherlands
William Louis of Nassau becomes governor/viceroy of Drenthe
Petition in Recife Brazil leads to closing of their 2 synagogues
Francesco Cavalli's opera "Giasone" premieres in Venice (the most popular opera of the 17th century)
Battle at Turkheim (Colmar): French army beats Brandenburg
Prussian King Frederik Willem I buys conscript for nobles
Britain, Hannover, Saxony-Poland and Austria sign anti-Prussian Russian pact
Failed assassination attempt on French King Louis XV by Damiens
Assembly of New Hampshire adopts its 1st state constitution
1st Swedenborgian temple in US holds 1st service in Baltimore
Ohio legislature passes 1st laws restricting free blacks movement
Treaty of Dardanelles concluded between Britain & France
Central America proclaims annexation to Mexican Empire
1st edition of Amsterdam General Trade Journal (Algemeen Handelsblad)
Kiowa Indians record this as the night the stars fell
Records show 95,820 licensed public houses in England on this date
James Clark Ross (UK) is 1st to enter pack ice near Ross Ice Shelf
The United States House of Representatives votes to stop sharing the Oregon Territory with the United Kingdom
California Exchange opens
Steamship San Francisco wrecked off US eastern seaboard, 300 die
1st steamboat the North Star sails up the northern Red River, America
250 Federal troops are sent from New York to Ft Sumter
Alabama troops seize Forts Morgan & Gaines at Mobile Bay
Charles Garnier's new Opera opens in Paris
President Grant sends federal troops to Vicksburg, Mississippi
1st US school of librarianship opens at Columbia University
Dutch Heidemaatschappij (land reclamation) established
1st successful auroral photograph made
Henry James' play "Guy Domville" opens in London
Victor Trumpermakes first-class debut for NSW 17 yrs 64 days
Isaac Albéniz' opera "Pepita Jiminez" premieres in Barcelona
Irish leader John Edward Redmond calls for a revolt against British rule
SF-Hawaii telegraph cable opens for public use
-34°F (-36.7°C), River Vale, New Jersey (state record)
-42°F (-41.1°C), Smethport, Pennsylvania (state record)
England beat Australia at the MCG, Rhodes 7-56 & 8-68
Charles Perrine announces discovery of Jupiter's 7th satellite, Elara
Incorporation of the National Association of Audubon Society, a nonprofit environmental organization dedicated to conservation
Colombia recognizes Panama's independence
San Francisco has its first air meet
1st National Hockey Association game (Victoria)
The Prague Party Conference takes place.
Austria-Hungary offensive against Montenegro
British premier Lloyd George demand for unified peace
German Workers' Party forms, precursor to the National Socialist German Workers' Party (Nazi)
Left-wing Spartacus organization instigates a revolt in Berlin; terrified by the spread of Bolshevism, German troops brutally suppress the uprising
French Baseball Federation awards silver medals to John McGraw, Charlie Comiskey, & Hugh Jennings
James Gleason & Richard Tabers "Is zat so?" premieres in NYC
Nellie Tayloe Ross sworn in as Governor of Wyoming, 1st woman governor in USA
Under Polish control, Danzig establishes Port Gdańsk post office
Fox Studios exhibits Movietone
Coup by King Alexander in South Slavia
First woman to purchase a baseball team, Lucille Thomas buys Topeka Senators franchise in the Western League
Work on Golden Gate Bridge begins, starting on the Marin County side
Boston's Fenway Park catches fire for 2nd time (May 8th 1926 also)
National & American baseball leagues select a uniform ball
Only unicameral state legislature in US opens 1st session in Nebraska
FCC hears the 1st transmission of FM radio with clear, static-free signal
Finnish offensive at Suomossalmi against Russia
British Australian troops conquer Bardia, Libya
55 German tanks reach North-Africa
Teams agrees to start season later due to WW II
William H Hastie, civilian aide to secretary of war, resigns to protest segregation in armed forces
The Daily Mail becomes the first transoceanic newspaper.
Pepe LePew debuts in Warner Bros cartoon "Odor-able Kitty"
Surprise attack on Liese-Aktion-office on Marnix St, Amsterdam
General Spoor orders cease-fire on Sumatra
Carson McCuller's "Member of the Wedding" premieres in NYC
British Prime MinisterWinston Churchillmakes an official visit to the US
KMSP TV channel 9 in Minneapolis-St Paul, MN (IND) 1st broadcast
"Bozo the Clown" live children's show premieres on TV
Continental League, a proposed third baseball major league, gets an assurance of congressional support from NY Senator Kenneth Keating
AFL Championship, Balboa Stadium, San Diego: San Diego Chargers beat Boston Patriots, 51-10; Chargers RB Keith Lincoln rushes for 206 yards, 2 TDs
KLXA (now KTBN) TV channel 40 in Fontana-San Ana, CA (IND) begins
Bollingen prize for poetry presented to John Berryman and Karl Shapiro
Neville Williams' "Chronology of the Expanding World" completed
USSR Venera 5 launches for 1st successful planet landing (Venus)
23,000 Belgian mine workers strike
American daytime soap opera "All My Children" premieres on ABC
KPTS TV channel 8 in Hutchinson-Wichita, Kansas (PBS) begins broadcasting
1st one-day international, Australia v England at the MCG
Globetrotters lose 100-99 to NJ Reds, ending 2,495-game winning streak
Largest crowd at Cleveland Arena (Cavs vs Lakers-11,178)
NYC transit fare rises from 30 cents to 35 cents
US President Nixon signs a bill for NASA to begin research on a manned space shuttle
Mali & Niger break diplomatic relations with Israel
Netherlands recognizes German DR
An earthquake in Lima, Peru, kills six people, and damages hundreds of houses
14 die when British freighter "Lake Illawarra" rams pylon bridge between Derwent & Hobart, Tasmania & ship sinks
Salyut 4 with crew of 2 is launched for 30 days
Cambodia is renamed "Democratic Kampuchea"
In retaliation for the Reavey and O'Dowd killings, the South Armagh Republican Action Force shoot dead 10 Protestant civilians after stopping their minibus in County Armagh
Late night television news program "Nightline" with Ted Koppel extended from 20 mins to 30 mins
Arkansas judge rules against obligatory teaching of creation
Discovery moves to launch pad for STS 51-C mission
Operation Moses, covert airlifting of about 8000 Ethiopian Jews from war-torn Sudan to Israel via Belgium ends
Surrogate Baby M case begins in Hackensack, New Jersey
2 French TV newsmen arrested for trying to plant fake bombs on 3 airlines at JFK airport in security test
Baseball signs $400M with ESPN, showing 175 games in 1990
J Donald Crump appointed 8th Commissioner of CFL
Edwin Jongejans of Netherlands wins 1-metre springboard diving title
Kevin Bradshaw of US Intl scores NCAA Div 1 record 72 pts
Oil tanker MV Braer runs aground on the coast of the Shetland Islands, spilling 84,700 tons of crude oil
Price is Right model Janice Pennington sues CBS for show accident
Washington state executes Westley Allan Dodd by hanging (first legal hanging in America since 1965)
Aleksandr Popov swims world record 100m free style (47.82)
Bill Fitchbecomes the 5th coach in NBA history to register 850 career victories with the LA Clippers' 95-93 home win over Philadelphia 76'ers at the LA Memorial Sports Arena
Lockheed C-140 Jetstar crashes at Isfahan, Iran, killing 18
Hamas operative Yahya Ayyash is killed by an Israeli-planted booby-trapped cell phone
Muralitharan no-balled for throwing in ODI v WI at the Gabba
"Juan Darien-Carnival Mass" closes at Vivian Beaumont NYC
"Love Thy Neighbor" closes at Booth Theater NYC
Ice storm knocks out electricity in Quebec & Ontario
Vandals decapitate Copenhagen's Little Mermaid statue in Langelinie Park
A report reveals that doctor and former GP Harold Shipman has potentially killed hundreds of patients

Eris, the largest known dwarf planet in the solar system, is discovered by the team of Michael E. Brown, Chad Trujillo, and David L. Rabinowitz using images originally taken on October 21, 2003, at the Palomar Observatory.
First batsman to ever score 1000 runs in a single innings in cricket - 15 year-old Mumbai schoolboy Pranav Dhanawade is 1009 not out
"Fire and Fury: Inside the Trump White House" by Michael Wolff is published by Henry Holt and Company
Kitwe in Zambia bans shaking hands and the sale of fresh food in attempt to prevent a cholera outbreak
North Korea accepts South Korean invitation for high-level talks
Chinese government report predicts China's population will peak at 1.44 billion in 2029 before declining
Orthodox Church of Ukraine granted independence from the Russian Orthodox Church by Ecumenical Patriarch Bartholomew
Chinese professor Zhang Yongzhen publishes the first SARS-CoV-2 genome map online, allowing health professionals worldwide to identify COVID-19
Iran pulls out of the 2015 nuclear deal, will not limit its uranium enrichment
86th Heisman Trophy Award: DeVonta Smith, Alabama (WR)
Daily new cases of COVID-19 top 60,000 in the UK as data reveals one in fifty in England had COVID-19 within the last week
In Georgia Senate run-off elections Raphael Warnock (D) defeats Kelly Loeffler (R) and Jon Ossoff (D) defeats David Perdue (R) to give Democrats control of the US Senate
Six Gulf states, including Saudi Arabia, sign an agreement to ease the blockage with Qatar, in place since 2017
Louisiana Governor John Bel Edwards pardons Homer Plessy for buying whites-only train ticket in 1892 (resulted in U.S. Supreme Court case Plessy v. Ferguson 1896)
State of emergency declared across Kazakhstan amid unrest and protests against fuel price rises, 164 killed in three days
An Alaskan Airlines Boeing 737 Max 9 makes emergency landing in Portland after a fuselage section blows out midair, with US regulator then grounding hundreds of planes
Nigerian film "A Tribe Called Judah", created by Funke Akindele, becomes the first Nollywood film to earn more than $1bn naira ($1.1m) domestically
New York becomes the first US city to introduce a congestion charge of $9 for Manhattan's central business district
DukeLudovico Sforza's troops reconquer Milan
PopeClement VIIforbids English KingHenry VIIIto remarry
British naval expedition led byBenedict Arnoldburns Richmond, Virginia
Davy Crockettarrives in Nacogdoches, Texas, to aid the revolution
Bedřich Smetana's first opera "Branibori v Cechach"/ "The Brandenburgers in Bohemia" premieres in Prague, Kingdom of Bohemia
Johannes Brahms' collection of lovesongs, "Liebeslieder Walzer" premieres
"Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde" byRobert Louis Stevensonpublished by Longmans, Green & Co.
French CaptainAlfred Dreyfus, convicted of treason, publicly stripped of his rank; later exonerated
"Die Presse" newspaper (Germany) publicly announcesWilhelm Röntgen's discovery of X-rays and their potential for new methods of medical diagnoses in a front-page article
IndustrialistHenry Fordannounces his $5 minimum per-day wage, doubling most workers pay from $2.40 for a 9hr day to $5 for an 8hr day
MLB CommissionerKenesaw Mountain Landisbegins 3-day public hearing on charges that 4 games played between Chicago & Detroit in 1917 had been thrown to White Sox
Bonnie ParkermeetsClyde Barrowfor the first time at Clarence Clay's house
Mao Zedongwrites the essay "A Single Spark Can Start a Prairie Fire" to criticize cadres not creating rural revolutionary base areas
"Cavalcade" based on the play byNoël Coward, directed byFrank Lloydand starring Diana Wynyard and Clive Brook premieres in New York (Best Production/Picture 1934)
Australian cricket batsmen Jack Fingleton (136) andDon Bradman(out 270 the following day) make then record stand of 346 for 6th wicket in 3rd Test vs England in Melbourne
US PresidentHarry Trumanlabels his administration the "Fair Deal"
Darius Milhaud's orchestral composition "West Point Suite", marking the US Military Academy's sesquicentennial, premieres at Carnegie Hall, NYC
Samuel Beckett's French-language play "En Attendant Godot (Waiting for Godot)" premieres in Paris
Dodgers'Jackie Robinsonannounces his retirement rather than be traded to the NY Giants
US PresidentEisenhowerasks Congress to send troops to the Middle East
Buddy Hollyreleases his last record "It Doesn't Matter"; he is killed in a plane crash 29 days later
The Beatles perform withPaul McCartneyon bass guitar for the 1st time, at Litherland Town Hall, in Lancashire, England
PopePaul VIvisits Jordan & Israel
Alexander Dubčeksucceeds Antonín Novotný as communist party leader of Czechoslovakia
DrBenjamin Spockindicted for conspiring to violate draft law
Fantasy Records releases "Bayou Country", the second album by American bandCreedence Clearwater Revival, featuring singles "Good Golly, Miss Molly" and "Proud Mary"
John Lennonand his wifeYoko Onogive a press conference in Aalborg, Denmark, revealing that they were only visiting
Body of former world heavyweight boxing champion Charles "Sonny" Liston (40) is found by his wife Geraldine at their Las Vegas home; he had been dead for an estimated 6 days; foul play suspected
President of BangladeshSheikh Mujibur Rahmanfreed by the newly elected President of Pakistan Zulfikar Ali Bhutto
Columbia Records releasesBruce Springsteen's debut album "Greetings from Asbury Park, N.J."
Raul Julia appears on "TheBob NewhartShow" in the episode "Oh, Brother"
Bülent Ecevitbecomes Prime Minister of Turkey for the 3rd time
Peter Sutcliffe, a truck driver confesses that he is the "Yorkshire Ripper" and murdered 13 women
Star centerDarryl Sittlerleaves the Toronto Maple Leafs on medical advice because of mental depression; trade request was taking too long
Bryan Trottierfailed on 9th Islander penalty shot
Brian Larascores his maiden Test century with 277 for the West Indies v Australia in drawn 3rd Test at the Sydney Cricket Ground
Former MLB playerReggie Jacksonelected to the National Baseball Hall of Fame
Miami Dolphins coachDon Shulaannounces his retirement
"Kinks" singer-songwriterRay Davies(59) shot in the leg while chasing a 25-year old mugger in New Orleans, Louisiana
Bill Cowherresigns as head coach of the Pittsburgh Steelers
Mike Shanahanis formally introduced as head coach of the Washington Redskins
Brazilian supermodel,Adriana Lima, named the most valuable Victoria's Secret Angel, modelling for the brand from 1999-2018

Golden State and Sacramento combine for 41 3-pointers in Warriors' 127-123 win; set new NBA record for 3s in a game;Stephen Curryhits 10 triples while Buddy Hield leads Kings with 8, Justin Jackson adds 5
Funeral for Pope EmeritusBenedict XVIpresided over byPope Francisin St. Peter’s Basilica, Rome
Pocahontasrevealed as the 12th great-grandmother of actorEdward Nortonon PBS's "Finding Your Roots"
Battle of Nancy: Swiss Confederacy led by René II decisively defeats the Duchy of Burgundy, 7,000+ killed including the Duke of BurgundyCharles the Bold
The Great Frost begins during the night, a sudden cold snap that remains Europe's coldest ever winter. Thousands are killed across the continent and crops fail in France.
Start of theSex Pistolsfinal tour in Atlanta, Georgia
Ōjin, 15th Emperor of Japan according to legend (270-310), born in Umi, Japan (d. 310) [birthdate disputed]
Richard, 1st Earl of Cornwall, born in Winchester Castle (d. 1272)
Francisco Suarez, Spanish theologian, born in Granada, Spain (d. 1617)
William Cobbold, British organist and composer (Chapel Royal), born in the UK (d. 1639)
Xu Xiake, Chinese geographer, born in Jiangsu, China (d. 1641)
Shah Jahan, 5th Mughal Emperor of India (1628-58) who built the Taj Mahal for his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal, born in Lahore, Mughal Empire (d. 1666)
Henry Lawes, English composer, born in Dinton, England (d. 1662)
Archduke Leopold Wilhelm of Austria, born in Wiener Neustadt, Austria (d. 1662)
Jacob Balthasar Schutz, German composer, born in the Free Imperial City of Nuremberg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1700)
Guru Gobind Singh, Tenth Guru of Sikhism, founder and General of Khalsa Army, social reformist and poet, born in Patna Sahib (d. 1708)
Antonio Lotti, Italian Baroque composer, born in Venice, Republic of Venice (d. 1740)
Simon Fraser, 11th Lord Lovat, Scottish Jacobite among those defeated at Culloden (last man beheaded in England), birthplace unknown (d. 1747)
Pietro Filippo Scarlotti, Italian composer, born in Rome, Papal States (d. 1750)
Giuseppe Galli-Bibiena, Italian architect and painter, born in Parma, Duchy of Parma and Piacenza (now Italy)  (d. 1757)
William Wildman Shute Barrington, British statesman, born in Becket, Massachusetts (d. 1793)
Paulus Constantijn la Fargue, Dutch cartoonist and painter, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1782)
Jacques Cathelineau, French royalist & army leader, born in Le Pin-en-Mauges, France (d. 1793)
Constanze Mozart, wife of Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart, born in Zell im Wiesental, Germany (d. 1842)
Jean Baptiste Say, French economist (Political Economics), born in Lyon, France (d. 1832)
Pieter Fontijn, Dutch painter and drawer, born in Dordrecht, Netherlands (d. 1839)
Fortunato Santini, Italian priest and composer, born in Rome, Papal States (d. 1861)
Stephen Decatur, American naval hero (War of 1812), born in Sinepuxent, Maryland (d. 1820)
Zebulon Pike, American brigadier general and explorer (Pike's Peak), born in Lamington, New Jersey (d. 1813)
Kaspar Ett, German organist and composer, born in Eresing, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1847)
André Henri Constant van Hasselt, Belgian poet (La Belgium), born in Maastricht, Meuse-Inférieure (d. 1874)
Auguste Mermet, French opera composer, born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1889)
Cyrus Hamlin, American educator/missionary (est Robert College, Turkey), born in Waterford, Maine (d. 1900)
Thomas Neville Waul, Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Sumter County, South Carolina (d. 1903)
Joseph Brevard Kershaw, Major General (Confederate Army), born in Camden, South Carolina (d. 1894)
William Rockstro [Rackstraw], English musicologist, arranger, and composer, born in North Cheam, Surrey, England (d. 1895)
August Valentine Kautz, German-American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Baden, Germany (d. 1895)
Roger Tichborne, missing U.K. heir who was the subject of the longest criminal trial in British history, birthplace unknown (d. c.1854)
William John Wills, English explorer of Australia, member of the Burke and Wills expedition, born in Totnes, United Kingdom (d. 1861)
Friedrich Richard Faltin, Finnish composer, born in Gdańsk, Poland (d. 1918)
Hendrik van Rijgersma, Dutch naturalist and physician, born in Lemmer, Netherlands (d. 1877)
Camille Jordan, French mathematician, born in Lyon, France (d. 1922)
John Doby Kennedy, Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Camden, South Carolina (d. 1896)
Rudolf Christoph Eucken, German idealist philosopher (Nobel 1908), born in Aurich, Germany (d. 1926)
Khristo Botev, Bulgarian poet and nationalist revolutionary, born in Kalofer, Bulgaria (d. 1876)
Same Steele, Canadian soldier, born in Purbrook, Canada West (d. 1919)
Theodoor Verstraete, Flemish painter and etcher, born in Ghent, Belgium (d. 1907)
Gustaf af Geijerstam, Swedish author (Boken om Lillebror), born in Hed socken (d. 1909)
DeWitt Bristol Brace, inventor (spectrophotometer), born in Wilson, New York (d. 1905)
Heinrich Gottlieb Noren, Austrian violinist and composer (Kaleidoscope), born in Graz, Austria (d. 1928)
Bob Caruthers, American baseball pitcher/outfielder (American Association wins & ERA leader 1885, wins leader 1880; St. Louis Browns, Brooklyn Bridegrooms), born in Memphis, Tennessee (d. 1911)
Julio Garavito Armero, Colombian astronomer, born in Bogotá, Colombia (d. 1920)
Frederick Converse, American composer (The Pipe of Desire), born in Newton, Massachusetts (d. 1940)
Hans Eppinger, Austrian physician, born in Prague, Czech Republic (d. 1946)
Nikolai Medtner, Russian pianist and composer (Skazi), born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1951)
Nikolay Andreyevich Roslavets, Russian composer, born in Surazh, Russia (d. 1944)
Herbert Bayard Swope, U.S. journalist who coined the term "Cold War", born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1958)
Humbert Wolfe, Italian-born British poet, born in Milan, Italy (d. 1940)
George "Rube" Foster, American baseball pitcher (World Series 1915, 16; no-hitter 1916; Boston Red Sox), born in Lehigh, Oklahoma (d. 1976)
Albert van Dalsum, Dutch actor and director (Wilton's Zoo; Little Rascal), born in Nieuwer-Amstel, Noord-Holland, Netherlands (d. 1971)

Cora Witherspoon, American actress (Quality Street The Bank Dick), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1957)
Anne Morrison Chapin, American playwright, actress, (The Wild Westcotts), and screenwriter (The Sailor Takes A Wife), born in Shoals, Indiana (d. 1967)
Francesca Bertini, Italian actress (Odette, Assunta Spina), born in Florence, Tuscany, Italy (d. 1985)
Friedrich Blume, German musicologist (Das Rassenproblem in der Musik), born in Schlüchtern, Germany (d. 1975)
Paramahansa Yogananda, Indian guru (Autobiography of a Yogi), born in Gorakhpur, India (d. 1952)
Zoltán Böszörmény, Hungarian Nazi politician and exponent of Fascism in Hungary, born in Budapest, Hungary (d. after 1945)
Elizabeth Cotten, American folk and blues guitarist and songwriter ("Freight Train", Shake Sugaree"), born in Carrboro, North Carolina (d. 1987)
Jeannette Piccard, American high-altitude balloonist & Episcopal priest, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1981)
Hendrik Algra, Dutch teacher, journalist, historian, and politician (ARP), born in Hurdegaryp, Netherlands (d. 1982)
Jack Wagner, American actor (The Razor's Edge, Under Two Flags, Jive Junction), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 1965)
Theo Mackeben, German pianist and composer (Lady Fanny, Bel Ami), born in Starogard Gdański, Poland (d. 1953)
Paula Ludwig, Austrian-German poet, born in Vorarlberg, Austria (d. 1974)
Yves Tanguy, French surrealist painter, born in Paris, France (d. 1955)
Henri Honoré d'Estienne d'Orves, French officer/resistance fighter, born in Verrières-le-Buisson, France (d. 1941)
Mario Scelba, Italian politician (Prime Minister 1954-55), born in Caltagirone, Italy (d. 1991)
Dorothea "Stella" Gibbons, English author (Cold Comfort Farm), born in London (d. 1989)
Pierre Palla, Dutch pianist and organist, born in Venlo, Netherlands (d. 1968)
Harold Gatty, Australian aviator, navigator with Wiley Post, born in Campbell Town, Australia (d. 1957)
Leighton Lucas, English composer and conductor, born in London, England (d. 1982)
Erica Morini, Austrian concert violinist, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1995)
Jeane Dixon [Lydia Pinckert], American celebrity psychic and astrologer (A Gift of Prophecy), born in Medford, Wisconsin (d. 1997)
Kathleen Kenyon, English archaeologist, 1st person to place date on remains of Jericho, born in London (d. 1978)
Anton Ingolic, Slavic author (After Splavih, After Prelomu), born in Spodnja Polskava, Slovenia (d. 1992)
Volmari Iso-Hollo, Finnish athlete (Olympic gold 3,000m steeplechase 1936), born in Ylöjärvi, Finland (d. 1969)
Willi Soukop, sculptor and member of Royal Academy of Arts, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1995)
George Dolenz [Jure Dolenc], Italian actor (Scared Stiff, Count of Monte Cristo), born in Trieste, Austria-Hungary (d. 1963)
Simon Nowell-Smith, British writer, collector, and librarian, born in Winchester, England (d. 1996)
Stephen Cole Kleene, American mathematician (Regular Expressions), born in Hartford, Connecticut (d. 1994)
Ed Widseth, American College Football Hall of Fame tackle (University of Minnesota; Pro Bowl 1938; NY Giants), born in Gonvick, Minnesota (d. 1998)
Hugh Brannum, American actor and singer (Captain Kangaroo - "Mr. Green Jeans"; The Fred Waring Show - "Lumpy"), born in Sandwich, Illinois (d. 1987)
Jack Lovelock, New Zealand athlete (Olympic gold 1,500m 1936), born in Crushington, New Zealand (d. 1949)
Jean-Pierre Aumont, French actor (Lili, Cat & Mouse, Happy Hooker), born in Paris, France (d. 2001)
Frank Pace Jr., US Secretary of Army (1950-53), born in Little Rock, Arkansas (d. 1988)
Jack Haig [John Cecil Coppin], English actor ('Allo, Allo!), born in London, England (d. 1989)
Nicolas de Staël, French-Russian painter, born in Saint Petersburg, Russia (d. 1955)
Arthur H. Robinson, Canadian-born cartographer, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2004)
Mabel Leigh, British potter (Shorter and Son), born in Coatesville, Indiana (d. 1971)
Uta Graf, German-American soprano and teacher, born in Karlsruhe, Baden-Württemberg, Germany; (d. 1995)
Francis L. Kellogg, U.S. diplomat and prominent socialite, born in New York City (d. 2006)
Reginald Smith Brindle, British composer, born in Cuerden, Lancashire, England (d. 2003)
Wieland Wagner, German opera director, born in Bayreuth, Germany (d. 1966)
Dal Richards, Canadian musician, bandmaster and composer (discovered Michael Bublé), born in Vancouver, British Columbia (d. 2015)[1]
Severino Gazzelloni, Italian flautist, born in Roccasecca, Italy (d. 1992)
Arturo Benedetti Michelangeli, Italian pianist, born in Brescia, Italy (d. 1995)
Mohammad Aslam Khokhar, Pakistani cricketer (Pak bat scored 16 & 18 in only test), born in Lahore, Pakistan (d. 2011)
Jean, Grand Duke of Luxembourg (1964-2000), born in Berg Castle, Luxembourg (d. 2019)
Admiral Sir Anthony Synnot, Chief of the Australian Defence Force, born in Corowa, Australia (d. 2001)
(Patrick) "Buddy" Attaway, American country and Cajun fiddler, and songwriter ("I'm Sitting On Top Of The World"), born in Shreveport, Louisiana (d. 1968)
Robert L. Bernstein, American publisher (Random House), born in New York City (d. 2019)
Sam Phillips, American record producer and founder of Sun Records (launched Elvis Presley's career), born in Florence, Alabama (d. 2003)
Virginia Halas McCaskey, American football executive (principal owner Chicago Bears 1983-2025), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2025)
Dr Gilbert Bogle, New Zealand-Australian scientist who died in the Bogle-Chandler case, born in Wanganui, New Zealand (d. 1963)
Lou Carnesecca, American collegiate HOF basketball coach (St. John's University, 1965-70 and 1973-92), born in New York City (d. 2024)
Wolf Morris, English actor (Decoy), born in Portsmouth, United Kingdom (d. 1996)
Claude (Buddy) Young, American NFL running back (Yankees, Texans, Colts), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1983)
Hosea Williams, American activist, born in Attapulgus, Georgia (d. 2000)
Joshua Benjamin Jeyaretnam, Singaporean opposition leader and former Secretary-General of Singapore's Worker's Party, born in Jaffna, British Ceylon (d. 2008)
Robert Earle, American TV host (GE College Bowl), born in Baldwin, New York (d. 2019)
William De Witt Snodgrass, American poet under the pseudonym S. S. Gardons, born in Beaver Falls, Pennsylvania (d. 2009)
Imtiaz Ahmed, Pakistani cricketer (Pakistan wicket-keeper in 41 Tests 1952-62), born in Lahore, Pakistan (d. 2016)
Qian Qichen, Chinese diplomat and vice premier, born in Tianjin (d. 2017)
Zulfikar Ali Bhutto, 4th President and 9th Prime Minister of Pakistan, born in Ratodero Taluka, Pakistan (d. 1979)
Wilbert Harrison, American R&B singer ("Kansas City"; "Let's Work Together"), born in Charlotte, North Carolina (d. 1994)
Edward Galven Givens Jr., Major USAF/astronaut, born in Quanah, Texas (d. 1967)

Frederick Charles Tillis, American composer (In the Spirt and the Flesh; Secrets of the African Baobob), jazz saxophonist, music educator (University of Massachusetts, 1970-97), and poet, born in Galveston, Texas (d. 2020)
Richard Hayes, American actor and singer (Name That Tune), born in Passaic, New Jersey (d. 2014)
Alfred Brendel, Austrian pianist (first to record Beethoven's complete works for solo piano), musicologist, and poet, born in Loučná nad Desnou, Czechoslovakia (d. 2025)[1]
Alvin Ailey, African-American dancer and choreographer (American Dance Theater), born in Rogers, Texas (d. 1989)
Joan Coxsedge (née Rocherster), Australian anti-war activist, politician (he Victorian Legislative Council , 1979-92), and artist, born in Ballarat, Australia (d. 2024)
John McVay, American NFL football coach (New York Giants 1976-78) and executive (AD University of Dayton 1972-73; GM San Francisco 49ers 1998-99), born in Bellaire, Ohio (d. 2022)
Walt Davis, American athlete (Olympic gold high jump 1952) and basketball center (NBA C'ship 1956, 58; Philadelphia Warriors, St. Louis Hawks), born in Beaumont, Texas (d. 2020)
(Laten) Johnny Adams, American blues, jazz and gospel singer (Reconsider Me), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1998)
Umberto Eco, Italian philosopher and author (Name of the Rose), born in Alessandria, Piedmont (d. 2016)
Eddy Pieters Graafland, Dutch soccer goalkeeper (47 caps; Ajax; European Cup 1970, Feyenoord), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 2020)

Phil Ramone [Rabinowitz], American 14-time Grammy Award-winning music producer (Burt Bacharach; Paul Simon; Billy Joel; Tony Bennett), and studio owner (co-founded A&R Recording), born in Cape Town, South Africa (d. 2013)
